title-group.article-title: A systematic review on mindfulness-based immersive interventions in depressive disorders

abstract.p: Depressive disorders are characterized by persistent negative affect, rumination, and impaired emotion regulation, contributing substantially to global disease burden. As recurrent rumination and negative attentional biases are central cognitive mechanisms of depression, interventions targeting these processes have gained increasing attention. Mindfulness-based interventions (MBIs) effectively treat depressive disorders but face adherence challenges, which immersive technology may resolve through multi-sensory stimulation and real-time feedback. This systematic review assesses the therapeutic potential of mindfulness-based immersive interventions (MBIIs, which combine immersive technologies with MBIs) for depressive disorders. Following PRISMA guidelines, we identified 37 eligible studies from 670 screened records. Across the included studies, a proportion of studies (69%) reported greater improvements in depressive and anxiety-related symptoms in MBII conditions compared with conventional therapies, while 78% of the trials reported improvements in psychological processes. A subset of trials reported greater feasibility and acceptance for immersive implementations compared with non-immersive approaches (67%), and 89% reported improvements in physiological outcomes. However, significant gaps remain, including substantial variability in sessions and VR configurations, insufficient active controls, and limited long-term validation. Future research is recommended to prioritize mechanistic studies to clarify neurocognitive pathways, standardize sessions, and evaluate cost-effectiveness.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Depression continues to be a major public health issue worldwide, affecting individual well-being, economic productivity, and suicide prevention efforts1–4. Psychotherapy, especially cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), is effective for treating depression; however, substantial limitations remain with respect to long-term outcomes and treatment adherence5. Mindfulness is the awareness that arises from paying attention in a particular way to the unfolding of experience6. The definition of mindfulness-based interventions (MBIs) is a structured psychological intervention method combined with mindfulness practice, aiming to promote mental health and emotional well-being by cultivating individuals’ non-judgmental awareness of the present moment7–9. In recent years, MBIs have emerged as a significant non-pharmacological intervention in the treatment of depression. For example, MBIs have been shown to reduce depressive symptoms10, improve emotion regulation11, and lower the risk of relapse12. A meta-analysis of mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (MBCT) reported robust effects for relapse prevention, whereas evidence for its efficacy in alleviating current depressive symptoms remains inconclusive, underscoring the need for further investigation of long-term outcomes and comparative effectiveness across therapeutic approaches13. In general, MBIs aim to cultivate mindfulness skills through structured sessions, focusing on promoting awareness, attention control, and emotional regulation12. The primary therapeutic targets of MBIs include reducing rumination, enhancing self-compassion, and increasing present-moment awareness, all of which are key to alleviating depressive symptoms11,14. However, there are challenges in the clinical application of traditional MBI approaches. For instance, the typical 8-week duration of standard MBI programs often leads to low treatment adherence, as patients may struggle to maintain consistent participation over such an extended schedule7,15. Furthermore, patients with depression frequently experience difficulties concentrating their attention on the task of MBIs14,16. Depressive symptoms, particularly rumination, can impair cognitive flexibility and make it challenging for patients to stay focused during mindfulness exercises17. Additionally, some studies have reported that patients may find MBI sessions monotonous, further reducing patients’ motivation and engagement in the therapeutic process18,19. These challenges highlight the need for innovation in MBIs to provide a more effective and engaging intervention program for depressive disorders.

Mindfulness-based immersive interventions (MBIIs)—integrating immersive technologies (core technological layer in the Fig. 1) with MBIs—represent a potential solution20. In this review, immersive technologies were defined as digital systems that provide users with perceptually enriched or spatially simulated environments and support embodied or multisensory interaction. MBIIs leverage multiple sensory modalities (visual, auditory, haptic) to amplify the effectiveness and personalization of MBI training21–25. By placing patients in digital environments that enhance attentional focus26, MBIIs counteract rumination27,28 through embodied presence—a state where multisensory engagement anchors awareness to the present29,30. For example, previous studies have suggested that VR-mediated environments can reduce anxiety and stress by facilitating experiential acceptance through biofeedback loops31,32 that reward nonjudgmental awareness33,34. Critically, MBIIs are increasingly combined with complementary evidence-based therapies (adjunctive therapeutic layer in the Fig. 1) to create multimodal synergies: Virtual environments incorporate exercise regimens promoting adjunctive symptom reduction35–37. Multisensory feedback systems (e.g., heart rate variability monitoring) provide objective biomarkers for real-time self-regulation, representing continuous and actionable feedback to enhance emotional and cognitive control38,39; CBT-based virtual scenarios seamlessly address maladaptive thoughts within mindfulness practices37,40. Hybrid approaches amplify physiotherapeutics (e.g., accelerated transcranial magnetic stimulation, accel-TMS) via synchronized neural modulation41 and strengthen attentional control through music-driven stimulation26. These integrations translate to measurable clinical outcomes (clinical outcomes layer in the Fig. 1), including evaluation on depressive/anxiety symptom alleviation42–44, change of psychological processes45,46, change in physiological signal (e.g., reduced HRV47,48), and clinical adaptation of MBIIs49,50. Collectively, MBIIs represent a paradigm-shifting approach that transcends traditional MBI limitations—harnessing immersive technology to deliver personalized, multi-mechanistic interventions with demonstrable efficacy.Fig. 1This conceptual framework illustrates the multidimensional impact of mindfulness-based immersive interventions (MBIIs) on depressive disorders, structured across three concentric layers.
Core technological layer (purple): Delineates immersive technologies enabling MBIIs—augmented reality (AR), virtual reality (VR), and mixed reality (MR). Adjunctive therapeutic layer (green): Highlights complementary evidence-based approaches that can be either: (a) digitally integrated within MBII environments (e.g., CBT elements in virtual scenarios), or (b) physically combined with MBIIs as hybrid interventions (e.g., exercise regimens paired with VR mindfulness). Includes psychotherapy (e.g., CBT), multisensory stimulation (e.g., music therapy), physiotherapeutics (e.g., accel-TMS), and behavioral strategies (e.g., exercise). Clinical outcomes layer (blue): Captures multidimensional benefits: symptom alleviation, psychological process modulation, physiological adaptation, and clinical engagement. This framework delineates how MBIIs synergize with complementary approaches while maintaining technological immersion as the foundational component.

Despite the promising holistic potential of MBIIs in depressive disorders, comparative evidence between MBIIs and classical therapies remains fragmented. For example, while MBIIs show promise (e.g., superior emotion regulation vs. audio-only meditation51), direct comparisons against structured psychotherapies52 or physiotherapeutics like accel-TMS41 remain scarce and methodologically inconsistent (e.g., mismatched controls53, underpowered trials54). This obscures whether MBIIs replace, augment, or synergize with first-line interventions. Moreover, heterogeneity in protocols (e.g., dosing, VR hardware55) and outcome measures56 precludes identification of why MBIIs succeed—limiting personalized application. Limited synthesis of subjective experiences, such as acceptability and presence of MBIIs57,58, that mediate adherence. Cost-prohibitive development55, absent session standardization59, and lack of long-term validation60 stall real-world adoption. These gaps fragment the evidence base and stall clinical translation—highlighting an urgent need for comprehensive synthesis to guide implementation.

To the best of our knowledge, this review represents a potential perspective on the design and effects of research methods in the field of MBIIs for depressive disorders, which has not been systematically discussed in existing literature. This paper addresses a critical evidence gap by investigating three primary goals: (1) to systematically synthesize and compare the therapeutic effects of MBIIs for depressive disorders across different control conditions and comparator interventions, including CBT, physiotherapeutic approaches, multisensory stimulation, and conventional MBIs (Section “Main results”); (2) to evaluate the methodological characteristics and experimental rigor of included studies, encompassing study design, intervention implementation and session structure, comparison methods, and outcome measurement strategies, in order to contextualize the robustness of reported findings (Sections “Selection and inclusion of studies”, “Methodological features of studies”, and “Evaluation in leveraging MBIIs for treating depressive disorders”); and (3) to integrate these empirical and methodological insights to identify strengths, clinical implications, and key challenges for future development of MBIIs in depressive disorders, thereby providing guidance for research design and practical application (Section “4Discussion”). Guided by PRISMA principles61, this paper contributes a systematic review to investigate MBIIs’ impact on depressive symptoms across clinical methodologies and provides a comprehensive analysis of design efficacy and comparative effectiveness. It contributes valuable insights into the field of digital mental health interventions and lays the groundwork for further direction on improving depression care through MBIIs.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This review followed the PRISMA guidelines61 to systematically assess the clinical applications of immersive technologies in MBIs for depressive disorders. The literature search and selection process was conducted with rigor to ensure comprehensiveness and transparency. This systematic review was prospectively registered in the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO; registration number: CRD420251273080).

sec.title: Search strategy and literature selection
sec.p:
A comprehensive literature search was conducted by the first author on April 29, 2025. The search included all eligible studies published between January 1, 2016, and April 29, 2025. No earlier studies were considered, as this time frame was predefined to capture contemporary research on immersive technologies in mental health. The search included multiple academic databases: PubMed, ProQuest, Cochrane, MEDLINE, CINAHL, and Scopus. Search terms were organized into three conceptual domains: (1) immersive technologies (e.g., “virtual reality”, “augmented reality”, “mixed reality”, “VR”, “AR”, “XR”, “MR”, “metaverse”, “digital twins”), (2) mindfulness-based interventions (e.g., “mindfulness”, “mindfulness-based interventions”, “meditation”, “mindful”), and (3) depressive disorders (e.g., “depressive”, “depression”, “major depressive disorder”, “MDD”, “clinical depression”). Within each domain, synonyms and related terms were combined using the Boolean operator OR, while the three domains were combined using AND to ensure that retrieved studies addressed all core concepts. Database-specific adaptations of search syntax were applied where necessary. Searches were conducted primarily in titles, abstracts, and keywords. In addition to database searches, supplementary manual searches were conducted using Google Scholar to identify potentially relevant studies not indexed in the selected databases, including early-stage, interdisciplinary, or technology-oriented publications. These manual searches primarily involved forward and backward citation tracking of included articles and key reviews. Two rounds of study selection, namely title or abstract screening and full-text screening, were performed by two researchers who independently evaluated each article based on the inclusion and exclusion criteria outlined in Table 1 based on the PICO (population, intervention, comparison, outcome) framework62. In case of any conflicts, the first author made the final decision.Table 1
Eligibility criteria based on the PICO (population, intervention, comparison, outcome) framework62
CriteriaPopulationInterventionComparisonOutcomeOther
Inclusionindividuals with depressive symptomsMBIs utilizing immersive technologiescontrol (e.g., TAU, WLC) or self-before-after control experiment.reduction in depressive symptoms or improvements in depression-related quality of lifejust-accepted literature
Exclusionindividuals without depressive symptomspractices not incorporating immersive technologiesexploratory or a descriptive experimentoutcomes not related to depressive symptomsnon-English literature.
MBIs mindfulness-based interventions, TAU treatment as usual, WLC wait list controls. Immersive technologies included virtual reality (VR), augmented reality (AR), mixed reality (MR), extended reality (XR), as well as related immersive digital environments (e.g., metaverse-based systems or digital twins) when used as part of mindfulness-based interventions.

sec.title: Data collection process
sec.p:
We used Covidence, a web-based systematic review tool, to manage the literature screening process. The initial screening process involved reviewing titles and abstracts, with each reviewer independently determining study inclusion or exclusion criteria as described above. To assess the level of agreement between the reviewers, we calculated the percentage agreement by dividing the number of studies both reviewers agreed on (included or excluded) by the total number of studies reviewed and then multiplying by 100. The percentage agreement was 83% at the title and abstract screening stage, indicating a high level of concordance between the reviewers. Discrepancies between reviewers were resolved through discussion. Data were extracted from the included studies based on the previous framework of systematic review63,64 and the topic of this paper, focusing on key elements such as subjects of the study (patient versus non-patient, sample size, and demographic characteristics), study design (randomized controlled trials, RCTs versus non-RCTs), type of implementation (intervention by AR, VR, or MR technologies), sessions of intervention (e.g., an 8-week intervention comprising two 45-min sessions per week), comparators (e.g., MBII groups versus mindfulness-based interventions group), and clinical outcomes (e.g., reductions in depressive symptoms, feasibility measured of MBIIs, improvements in mindfulness and quality of life). General information, such as the title of the paper, first author, and year of publication, and answers to the questions listed in Table 2 were extracted.Table 2
Extraction questions based on the previous framework of systematic review63,64, and the topic of this paper
No.Extraction questions (EQ)

Note: MBIIs: mindfulness-based immersive interventions.

sec.title: Qualitative methods
sec.p: Qualitative approaches were critical for capturing participants’ subjective experiences and intervention acceptability. Data collection included: (1) post-intervention surveys with open-ended questions65 assessing perceived usefulness, enjoyment, and benefits of MBIIs; (2) thematic and textual narrative synthesis to identify emergent patterns from qualitative responses, particularly regarding the impact of MBIIs on well-being dimensions66.

sec.title: Quantitative methods
sec.p: Quantitative methods offered objective measures for assessing the effectiveness of MBIIs, including both primary and secondary outcomes. Primary outcomes were assessed on depression or anxiety. These were evaluated using standardized scales including Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-967), Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS68), Beck Depression Inventory (BDI69), Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAM-D70), Behavioral Activation for Depression Scale-Short Form (BADS-SF71), 30-item Geriatric Depression Scale (GDS-3072), Beck Anxiety Inventory (BAI73), State-Trait Anxiety Inventory-6 (STAI-674), Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale Depression subscale (HADS-D75), Generalized Anxiety Disorder-7 (GAD-776), and Psychotic Symptom Rating Scales-Delusions (PSYRATS-D77).

Secondary outcomes were assessed across three dimensions: psychological processes, feasibility and clinical adaption, and physiological measures. Psychological processes of change, including motivation, mindfulness, attention control, emotion regulation, self-awareness, and well-being were evaluated using standardized scales including Five Facets Mindfulness Questionnaire Short Form (FFMQ-SF78), Toronto Mindfulness Scale (TMS79), Motivation and Pleasure Scale-Self Rating (MAP-SR80), Positive and Negative Affect Schedule (PANAS81), Difficulties in Emotion Regulation Scale (DERS82), Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS83), Multidimensional Assessment of Interoceptive Awareness (MAIA84), Brunel Mood Scale (BRUMS85), Emotional Discomfort Detection Scale (DME86), Addenbrooke’s Cognitive Examination-III (ACE-III87), and Short Warwick-Edinburgh Mental Well-Being Scale (SWEMWBS88). Feasibility and clinical application of MBII programs, encompassing usability, user experience, immersion, and cognitive load were measured through System Usability Scale (SUS89), Virtual Reality Sickness Questionnaire (VRSQ90), Perceived Stress Scale (PSS-1091), Simulator Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ92), Igroup Presence Questionnaire (IPQ93), User Experience Questionnaires (UEQ94), and NASA Task Load Index (NASA-TLX95). Physiological measures96–98 to complement quantitative outcomes included electroencephalogram (EEG), electromyography (EMG), galvanic skin response (GSR), and heart rate variability (HRV).

sec.title: Evaluation metrics
sec.p: Key statistical indicators included significance thresholds, measures of central tendency and dispersion, effect size estimates, and regression coefficients to evaluate predictors of intervention outcomes. These metrics assessed the magnitude, precision, and clinical relevance of observed effects while controlling for potential confounders.

sec.title: Assessment of methodological rigor and study quality
sec.p: In line with the aims of this review, methodological rigor was assessed using a structured narrative synthesis. This approach is consistent with prior systematic reviews in digital mental health99,100 and human-computer interaction101,102 research, where substantial heterogeneity in study designs, intervention modalities, and outcome measures limits the applicability of standardized bias tools. Specifically, study quality was evaluated across four predefined methodological domains: (1) study design characteristics, including randomized versus non-randomized designs, presence and type of control conditions, and sample size adequacy; (2) intervention specification, including clarity of mindfulness components, immersive technology configurations, session duration, and intervention dose; (3) outcome measurement rigor, including the use of validated clinical scales for depressive symptoms and psychological processes, as well as the integration of objective or physiological measures; and (4) analytic transparency, including reporting of statistical methods, handling of confounders, and completeness of outcome reporting. Rather than generating a composite quality score, these domains were used to identify recurring methodological strengths and limitations across studies. Studies with unvalidated outcome measures, insufficiently described interventions, or inadequate control of confounding factors were excluded during full-text screening. Among included studies, variability in sample size, control conditions, and outcome selection was systematically documented and synthesized to contextualize the strength of the evidence and inform interpretation of findings.

sec.title: Selection and inclusion of studies
sec.p:
A total of 670 articles were initially identified, with 650 retrieved from database searches and an additional 20 found through manual searches on Google Scholar (Fig. 2). After removing 219 duplicates, 451 articles remained for screening. These articles were reviewed through title and abstract screening based on the eligibility criteria (Table 1), resulting in the selection of 94 articles for full-text review. Subsequently, we carefully read the full text of each article and excluded 57 articles. 22 articles were excluded due to insufficient methodological rigor (i.e, 14 unvalidated outcome measures, 16 articles with unclear intervention descriptions, and 2 articles lacking control for confounding factors); 14 articles were excluded due to the lack of participants with depressive symptoms; 12 articles were excluded since they did not involve mindfulness-related exercises or did not use immersive technologies; 7 articles were excluded since they were merely exploratory or descriptive studies; and 2 articles were excluded because they were not in English. Following a rigorous evaluation, 37 studies were included in the final analysis.Fig. 2This flowchart illustrates the systematic study selection process following PRISMA guidelines.
Beginning with 650 records from six academic databases and 20 additional records from Google Scholar, we conducted duplicate removal, title or abstract screening, and full-text assessment. Ultimately, 37 studies met the inclusion criteria for this review. The detailed methodology is described in the Methods section.

sec.title: Study design and sample characteristics
sec.p:
As shown in Table 3, 18 of the 37 included studies were RCTs, while the remaining 19 employed non-RCT designs. Non-RCT studies comprised single-blind or double-blind trials as well as single-arm designs. Although RCTs were commonly used, their methodological characteristics varied across studies. For instance, Ritvo et al. employed a parallel-arm RCT with intention-to-treat and per-protocol analyses to evaluate an 8-week Mindfulness Virtual Community (MVC) program for undergraduates. However, a concurrent university labor strike introduced confounding stressors, potentially biasing outcomes such as depression (PHQ-9) and anxiety (BAI) scores103.Table 3

Sessions of intervention are categorized as follows BI brief interventions where participants experienced only one session (10 min ≤ duration ≤ 45 min), STI short-term interventions (1 day ≤ duration ≤ 2 weeks, 10–45 min per session), MTI mid-term interventions (2 weeks < duration ≤ 4 weeks, 10–45 min per session), and LTI long-term interventions (duration > 4 weeks, 10–45 min per session).
More detailed descriptions can be found in the online supplementary table.

Sample sizes ≤ 60 participants were prevalent (25 studies, 67.6%), with 12 studies (32.4%) having n ≤ 20. Pilot designs prioritized feasibility over generalizability. Studies such as Senda et al. (n = 15)41 and Van et al. (n = 20)104 prioritized feasibility over generalizability, limiting their statistical power to detect small-to-moderate effects. Rodrigues et al. further highlighted pandemic-related recruitment challenges, with only 44 COVID-19 patients enrolled across four sites, which restricted subgroup analyses in their multicenter trial53.

Across the included studies, participants were broadly categorized into two groups: individuals with clinically diagnosed conditions accompanied by depressive symptoms (n = 1027), including populations such as patients with cancer, schizophrenia, or depressive disorders; and non-clinical participants reporting depressive symptoms (n = 1222), among whom college students and adolescents constituted a substantial proportion. Populations ranged from adolescents (e.g., mean age 17.03105) to elderly cohorts (e.g., mean age 70.73106), with interventions tailored to developmental needs (e.g., gamification for youth versus low-intensity VR for seniors). Cultural specificity was also considered, as seen in studies like Mao et al.107, which incorporated localized content (e.g., income tiers in yuan), and Rowland et al.108, which focused on Indigenous cultural contexts. Moreover, some trials included participants with overlapping diagnoses (e.g., depression, PTSD, and chronic pain41), further complicating the isolation of MBII effects.

However, narrow inclusion criteria (e.g., only two spinal cord injury patients in the study109) limit the extrapolation of findings to broader depressive populations. Furthermore, heterogeneous baselines, such as variability in depression severity (e.g., PHQ-9 scores ranging from mild to severe57), obscure session-response relationships. Additionally, geographic representation showed Western dominance (24 studies, 64.9%), followed by Asian populations (12 studies, 32.4%), with only 1 study (2.7%) from a Global South region. The uneven distribution of the studied population was emphasized.

Figure 3 presents a histogram summarizing the implementations of MBIIs for depressive disorders across the 37 included studies. The figure also depicts the distribution of intervention session characteristics, including duration and frequency. These implementation patterns are described in detail below.Fig. 3Histogram of various comparators of mindfulness-based immersive interventions (MBIIs) on depressive disorders based on 37 reviewed studies.
Study groups included: MBII groups versus mindfulness-based interventions (MBIs) groups, MBII groups versus physicotherapeutics groups, MBII groups versus psychotherapy groups, MBII groups versus multisensory stimulation groups, MBII groups versus other approaches groups, which consists of treatment as usual (TAU), wait-list controls (WLC), and a physical room (PR) as the control condition, and MBIIs (without control groups) that did not include a control group.

sec.title: Implementations of intervention
sec.p: Across the included trials, interventions exhibited substantial technological heterogeneity. As shown in Table 3, 26 studies used VR-based mindfulness (VRM), 9 studies combined VRM with other clinical approaches (VRM+), 1 study employed AR-based mindfulness mixed with neurofeedback (ARM+), and 1 study utilized MR-based mindfulness (MRM) as the intervention type. Interventions were delivered in two forms: guided and self-guided. For example, Lee et al.110 used HeartMath biofeedback-integrated immersive VRM for real-time emotional coherence monitoring, whereas Van et al.104 relied on self-guided VR mindfulness without feedback. Content customization also varied widely. Mao et al.107 incorporated AI-driven emotion tracking and personalized mindfulness courses, while Habak et al.111 used a fixed MR environment to evoke future-oriented hope.

sec.title: Sessions of intervention
sec.p: The sessions of interventions also exhibited considerable variability. Drawing on historical research frameworks in this domain, we conducted a systematic classification of extant studies112–115 based on parameters (e.g., duration) of technology-mediated mindfulness interventions. The studies were categorized into four types (Table 3): 16 studies were brief interventions (BI), where participants experienced only one session (10 min ≤ duration ≤ 45 min), 6 studies were short-term interventions (STI, 1 day ≤ duration ≤ 2 weeks, 10–15 min per session), 6 studies were mid-term interventions (MTI, 2 weeks < duration ≤ 4 weeks, 10–45 min per session), and 9 studies were long-term interventions (LTI, duration > 4 weeks, 10–15 min per session). The interventions ranged from single 10-min sessions51 to 10-week programs116. While shorter sessions (e.g., 4 weeks107,) prioritized feasibility, they may lack sustained therapeutic impact, whereas longer programs risk participant attrition. For example, BI reported immediate anxiolytic effects but showed minimal durability53,117. In contrast, LTI achieved sustained symptom reduction118, yet faced higher attrition due to cognitive burden in depressed populations119. While MTI interventions balanced feasibility and impact108, only LTI induced measurable neurocognitive changes (e.g., increased dlPFC activation on fMRI120). Acute depressive episodes benefited most from BI’s rapid symptom relief (e.g., VR crisis intervention110), whereas chronic depression required LTI’s skill consolidation. Notably, little study systematically explored session-response effects, underscoring a critical gap for future research.

sec.title: Outcome measures
sec.p: The reliance on self-reported psychometric scales (e.g., PHQ-9, HADS, FFMQ-SF) across studies introduces potential response biases that may confound outcome interpretation. While subjective measures remain pragmatic for assessing depressive symptoms, only a minority of trials supplemented these with objective biomarkers, such as HRV and GSR, which provide quantifiable physiological correlates of emotional regulation115,117. Furthermore, the temporal scope of most investigations was constrained by short-term intervention (≤4 weeks), with exceptions such as extending assessments to 8 weeks post-intervention59. Even in studies reporting sustained symptom reductions (e.g., demonstrating improvements in the GDS at 2-week follow-up106), the absence of longitudinal data beyond acute phases limits conclusions about durability—a critical gap given the recurrent nature of depression.

sec.title: Main results
sec.p:
The impact of MBIIs on depressive disorders in all studies was primarily categorized into seven comparators (Fig. 3). Specifically, there were 2 studies comparing MBII groups versus physicotherapeutics groups39,41, 2 studies comparing MBII groups versus exercise groups54,120, 2 studies comparing MBII groups versus psychotherapy groups52,106, 6 studies comparing MBIIs versus multisensory stimulation groups53,57,121–124, 7 studies comparing MBII groups versus MBI groups51,115,117,118,125,126, 9 studies comparing MBII groups versus other control groups (i.e., Treatment as Usual (TAU); Wait List Control (WLC), or physical room)58,103,104,110,116,119,127–129 and 9 studies that did not include a control group (without control groups)105,107–109,111,130–133. The statistical results of all findings related to the comparators are summarized in Table 4.Table 4
